meaning: used before countable or singular nouns referring to people or things that have not already been mentioned
used to express surprise, pleasure or sympathy, or when you disagree with somebody
the whole number of
examples: All horses are animals, but not all animals are horses.

meaning: used to check that somebody agrees or understands
examples: We've got to get up early, all right?

meaning: used for introducing a statement that makes the main statement in a sentence seem surprising
examples: Although the sun was shining, it wasn't very warm.

meaning: also; in addition to
one more; an extra thing or person

used with uncountable or plural nouns in negative sentences and questions, after if or whether, and after some verbs such as prevent, ban, forbid, etc. to refer to an amount or a number of something, however large or small
used instead of somebody in negative sentences and in questions after if/whether, and after verbs such as prevent, forbid, avoid, etc.
examples: Is there anybody who can help me?

meaning: used instead of someone in negative sentences and in questions after if/whether, and after verbs such as prevent, forbid, avoid, etc.
used instead of something in negative sentences and in questions; after if/whether; and after verbs such as prevent, ban, avoid, etc.
used in negative sentences and in questions instead of somewhere
examples: I don't have anywhere to stay.

meaning: while something else is happening
examples: He sat watching her as she got ready.

meaning: used for asking people to listen to something that is being announced
examples: Attention, please! Passengers for flight KL412 are requested to go to gate 21 immediately.

meaning: for the reason that
examples: I did it because he told me to.

meaning: used with plural nouns to mean ‘the two’ or ‘the one as well as the other’
used to introduce a word or phrase that contrasts with what was said before
examples: I got it wrong. It wasn't the red one but the blue one.

meaning: used in expressions that show that you are surprised, upset, annoyed or worried
examples: Oh dear! I think I've lost my purse!

meaning: a group of twelve of the same thing
used to refer to every one of two or more people or things, when you are thinking about them separately
one or the other of two; it does not matter which
examples: You can park on either side of the street.

meaning: used before plural or uncountable nouns to mean ‘as many or as much as somebody needs or wants’
used with singular nouns to refer to all the members of a group of things or people
examples: She knows every student in the school.

meaning: every person; all people
every person; all people
all things
used before you mention something that makes a statement not completely true
examples: I didn't tell him anything except that I needed the money.

meaning: used with plural nouns and a plural verb to mean ‘a small number’, ‘some’
a male person or animal that has already been mentioned or is easily identified
examples: Everyone liked my father—he was the perfect gentleman.

meaning: of or belonging to a woman or girl who has already been mentioned or is easily identified
of or belonging to her
used when the woman or girl who performs an action is also affected by it
used to attract somebody’s attention or to express interest, surprise or anger
examples: Hey, can I just ask you something?

meaning: used as the object of a verb, after the verb be or after a preposition to refer to a male person or animal that has already been mentioned or is easily identified
used when the man or boy who performs an action is also affected by it
of or belonging to a man or boy who has already been mentioned or is easily identified
used as the subject of a verb when the speaker or writer is referring to himself/herself
examples: I think I'd better go now.

meaning: used to say that one thing can, will or might happen or be true, depending on another thing happening or being true
examples: If you see him, give him this note.

meaning: used to refer to an animal or a thing that has already been mentioned or that is being talked about now
belonging to or connected with a thing, an animal or a baby
examples: Turn the box on its side.

meaning: used when the animal or thing that does an action is also affected by it
happening or coming after all other similar things or people
examples: We caught the last bus home.

meaning: smallest in size, amount, degree, etc.
examples: He's the best teacher, even though he has the least experience.

meaning: used with uncountable nouns to mean ‘a smaller amount of’
used with uncountable nouns to mean ‘a small amount’, ‘some’
a large number or amount of somebody/something

used with plural nouns and verbs, especially in negative sentences or in more formal English, to mean ‘a large number of’. Also used in questions to ask about the size of a number, and with ‘as’, ‘so’ and ‘too’.
examples: We don't have very many copies left.

meaning: the form of I that is used when the speaker or writer is the object of a verb or preposition, or after the verb be
of or belonging to the person writing or speaking
a larger number or amount of
the largest in number or amount
examples: Who do you think will get (the) most votes?

meaning: used with uncountable nouns, especially in negative sentences to mean ‘a large amount of something’, or after ‘how’ to ask about the amount of something. It is also used with ‘as’, ‘so’ and ‘too’.
examples: I don't have much money with me.

meaning: of or belonging to the speaker or writer
used when the speaker or writer is also the person affected by an action
examples: I cut myself on a knife.

meaning: not one nor the other of two things or people
not one; not any; not a
examples: No student is to leave the room.

meaning: not anyone; no person
not anyone; no person
not one of a group of people or things; not any
and not
examples: She seemed neither surprised nor worried.

meaning: not anything; no single thing
examples: There was nothing in her bag.

meaning: used when you are reacting to something that has been said, especially if you did not know it before
examples: ‘I saw Ben yesterday.’ ‘Oh yes, how is he?’

meaning: yes; all right
as soon as; when
examples: We didn't know how we would cope once the money had gone.

meaning: used to introduce another possibility
examples: Is your sister older or younger than you?

meaning: used to refer to people or things that are additional or different to people or things that have been mentioned or are known about
examples: Mr Harris and Mrs Bate and three other teachers were there.

meaning: belonging to us; connected with us
the one or ones that belong to us
examples: Their house is very similar to ours, but ours is bigger.

meaning: the reflexive form of we; used when you and another person or other people together cause and are affected by an action
examples: We shouldn't blame ourselves for what happened.

meaning: used to emphasize that something belongs to or is connected with somebody
a large amount; as much or as many as you need
used to add more information
examples: I've got too much on at work. Plus my father is not well.

meaning: more than two but not very many
a female person or animal that has already been mentioned or is easily identified
examples: ‘What does your sister do?’ ‘She's a dentist.’

meaning: used to show the reason for something
examples: It was still painful, so I went to see a doctor.

meaning: used with uncountable nouns or plural countable nouns to mean ‘an amount of’ or ‘a number of’, when the amount or number is not given
examples: There's some milk in the fridge.

meaning: a person who is not known or mentioned by name
a person who is not known or mentioned by name
a thing that is not known or mentioned by name
examples: We stopped for something to eat.

meaning: a place that you do not know or do not mention by name
examples: We need to find somewhere to live.

meaning: used to emphasize the great degree of something
examples: This issue was of such importance that we could not afford to ignore it.

meaning: used to introduce the second part of a comparison
used after some verbs, adjectives and nouns to introduce a new part of the sentence
examples: She said (that) the story was true.

meaning: used to refer to somebody/something that has already been mentioned or is easily understood
examples: There were three questions. The first two were relatively easy but the third one was hard.

meaning: of or belonging to people, animals or things that have already been mentioned or are easily identified
of or belonging to them
examples: Theirs are the children with very fair hair.

meaning: used when referring to people, animals or things as the object of a verb or preposition, or after the verb be
used when people or animals performing an action are also affected by it
examples: They seemed to be enjoying themselves.

meaning: people, animals or things that have already been mentioned or are easily identified
examples: ‘Where are John and Liz?’ ‘They went for a walk.’

meaning: used to refer to a particular person, thing or event that is close to you, especially compared with another
examples: How long have you been living in this country?

meaning: despite the fact that
examples: Anne was fond of Tim, though he often annoyed her.

meaning: until
used to say that something can only happen or be true in a particular situation
examples: You won't get paid for time off unless you have a doctor's note.

meaning: used when the speaker or writer and another or others are the object of a verb or preposition, or after the verb be
examples: She gave us a picture as a wedding present.

meaning: I and another person or other people; I and you
used in questions to ask for particular information about somebody/something
any or every; anything or everything
at or during the time that
examples: I loved history when I was at school.

meaning: at any time that; on any occasion that
examples: You can ask for help whenever you need it.

meaning: (in) the place or situation in which
used to compare or contrast two facts
examples: Some of the studies show positive results, whereas others do not.

meaning: in any place
used to express a doubt or choice between two possibilities
examples: He seemed undecided whether to go or stay.

meaning: used in questions to ask somebody to be exact about one or more people or things from a limited number
examples: Which is better exercise—swimming or tennis?

meaning: during the time that something is happening; at the same time as something else is happening
examples: We must have been burgled while we were asleep.

meaning: used in questions to ask about the name, identity or function of one or more people
used instead of ‘who’ as the object of a verb or preposition
used in questions to ask who something belongs to
used to show that you are very surprised or impressed by somebody/something
yes (informal)
used to answer a question and say that something is correct or true
despite what has just been said
examples: It's a small car, yet it's surprisingly spacious.

meaning: used as the subject or object of a verb or after a preposition to refer to the person or people being spoken or written to
examples: You said you knew the way.

meaning: of or belonging to the person or people being spoken or written to
of or belonging to you
used when the person or people being spoken to both cause and are affected by an action
